# Configuration Density

## The Physics

The ratio of configurable behavior to hardcoded behavior determines a system's
adaptability. High configuration density means behavior changes through settings,
not through rebuilding. Low configuration density means every change requires
engineering.

This applies to organizations as much as software: a governance system where rules
are configurable per venture is more adaptable than one where rules are baked into
the structure.

## Why It Matters

- **Adaptability**: New ventures, new contexts, and new requirements are configuration changes, not redesigns.
- **Speed**: Configuration changes are fast. Structural changes are slow.
- **Personalization**: Each venture can tune governance to its context without forking the system.
- **Testing**: Configuration changes can be validated without rebuilding.

## Academic/Industry Backing

- 12-Factor App methodology: config in the environment, not the code.
- Feature flag patterns (LaunchDarkly, Split): behavior from configuration.
- Policy-as-code (OPA, Sentinel): governance rules as configurable policies.

## Connected Objects

- JOS-P10 (Deliberate Creation) — configuration is set deliberately, not inherited silently
- JOS-C01 (Fractal Self-Similarity) — configuration patterns repeat at every scale
